semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for camera housings with lenses and mounting brackets, often near ceilings or entrances. Use object detection for CCTV or camera shapes. Confirm a lens or dome form to avoid confusion with speakers. Consider typical placement patterns for surveillance devices.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use object metadata for cameras and their mount points. Tag when surveillance camera objects are visible in the primary field of view. Check orientation and placement near access points for additional confirmation.

env.ae.text/information_density_high.definition: Article Eater antecedent: Text/Information Density High

semantics.definition_short: Large amounts of visible text or information are present, creating a dense informational field.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the scene contains many text elements or densely packed informational displays. This includes signage clusters, wall graphics with extensive text, or multiple posters and notices. Apply when text density is visually dominant. Do not use for a single sign or sparse labeling. This tag is about text volume, not branding alone.
semantics.definition: Use when the scene contains many text elements or densely packed informational displays. This includes signage clusters, wall graphics with extensive text, or multiple posters and notices. Apply when text density is visually dominant. Do not use for a single sign or sparse labeling. This tag is about text volume, not branding alone.
examples_positive: A bulletin board covered with many notices and flyers.
A transit wall with multiple schedules and maps.
Clear evidence of Text / Information Density High is present.
examples_negative: A single exit sign on an otherwise blank wall.
A logo sign with minimal text.
No clear evidence of Text / Information Density High is present.
scope_includes: clusters of signs
dense posters
multi-panel information boards
text-heavy wall graphics
scope_excludes: single signs
minimal labeling
text-free surfaces
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Use OCR or text detection to count text regions and measure area coverage. High density shows many text boxes across the frame. Confirm that text is spatially distributed and visually dominant. Avoid tagging if text is small, distant, or limited to one corner.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use scene graph labels for sign objects and text textures. Compute text area coverage in the view. Tag when text objects exceed a density threshold across main surfaces.

semantics.definition_short: Textiles or soft furnishings such as curtains, rugs, or fabric elements are visibly present.
semantics.definition_long: Use when fabric-based elements are clearly visible and contribute to the scene. This includes curtains, drapes, rugs, upholstered panels, or fabric decor. Apply when textiles are a noticeable feature, not just minor accents. Do not use for spaces with only hard finishes and no visible fabrics. This tag is about soft furnishing presence.
semantics.definition: Use when fabric-based elements are clearly visible and contribute to the scene. This includes curtains, drapes, rugs, upholstered panels, or fabric decor. Apply when textiles are a noticeable feature, not just minor accents. Do not use for spaces with only hard finishes and no visible fabrics. This tag is about soft furnishing presence.
examples_positive: A living room with curtains and a large rug.
An office with fabric wall panels and upholstered seats.
Clear evidence of Textiles / Soft Furnishings is present.
examples_negative: A room with only metal and glass surfaces.
A space with no visible fabric elements.
No clear evidence of Textiles / Soft Furnishings is present.
scope_includes: curtains and drapes
rugs and carpets
upholstered panels
fabric decor
scope_excludes: hard-only interiors
minimal fabric accents
non-textile materials
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Detect fabric textures and soft materials such as curtains, rugs, and upholstered elements. Look for draped forms and textile patterns. Ensure textiles are visible and contribute to the scene composition. Avoid tagging if fabric elements are tiny or out of focus.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material classifications for fabrics and soft furnishings. Tag when textile materials occupy noticeable visible area. Consider the number and size of fabric objects in the view.

semantics.definition_short: Lighting is even and consistent across the space with minimal hotspots or shadows.
semantics.definition_long: Use when illumination appears evenly distributed, with similar brightness across surfaces and few strong shadows. The space should feel uniformly lit rather than dramatic or spotlit. Apply to offices, retail, or rooms with diffuse ambient lighting. Do not use when lighting contrast is high or uneven. This tag emphasizes lighting uniformity.
semantics.definition: Use when illumination appears evenly distributed, with similar brightness across surfaces and few strong shadows. The space should feel uniformly lit rather than dramatic or spotlit. Apply to offices, retail, or rooms with diffuse ambient lighting. Do not use when lighting contrast is high or uneven. This tag emphasizes lighting uniformity.
examples_positive: An office with evenly lit ceiling panels throughout.
A store with uniform ambient lighting across aisles.
Clear evidence of Uniform Lighting is present.
examples_negative: A gallery with strong spotlights and dark corners.
A room with bright window patches and deep shadows.
No clear evidence of Uniform Lighting is present.
scope_includes: diffuse ambient lighting
even overhead lighting
consistent brightness
low shadow contrast
scope_excludes: spotlighting
high contrast lighting
localized bright pools
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Measure luminance variance across the frame and check for low contrast between regions. Uniform lighting shows a narrow brightness distribution. Confirm that shadows and hotspots are minimal. Avoid tagging if daylight creates strong gradients.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use illuminance sampling to compute variance across the floor and walls. Tag when illuminance is relatively even and contrast ratios are low. Identify absence of strong directional light gradients.

env.ae.view_to_outdoors.definition: Article Eater antecedent: View to Outdoors

semantics.definition_short: The scene provides a visible view to the outdoors through windows or openings.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the exterior environment is clearly visible through windows, doors, or open facades. The view should show outdoor elements such as sky, vegetation, or buildings. Apply even if the opening is closed, as long as the outdoor view is visible. Do not use for interior-only views or opaque glazing. This tag focuses on visual connection to outside.
semantics.definition: Use when the exterior environment is clearly visible through windows, doors, or open facades. The view should show outdoor elements such as sky, vegetation, or buildings. Apply even if the opening is closed, as long as the outdoor view is visible. Do not use for interior-only views or opaque glazing. This tag focuses on visual connection to outside.
examples_positive: A room with windows showing trees and sky.
A lobby with glass doors revealing the outside street.
Clear evidence of View to Outdoors is present.
examples_negative: A windowless interior room.
A space with frosted glass that blocks the view.
No clear evidence of View to Outdoors is present.
scope_includes: clear windows to exterior
openings with outdoor view
glass doors showing outside
atrium openings to outside
scope_excludes: opaque or frosted glazing
interior glass partitions
no exterior view
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Detect window regions and confirm visible outdoor content beyond them. Use scene classification to identify sky, vegetation, or exterior structures. Avoid tagging when glazing is opaque or reflective without a view. Ensure the outdoor view is not a screen or image.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use scene graph adjacency to exterior zones and check visibility through openings. Tag when the camera has a line of sight to an exterior environment. Confirm that the view is to outside geometry, not a display texture.

semantics.definition_short: Visible dirt, grime, or decay is present on surfaces or objects.
semantics.definition_long: Use when dirt, stains, mold, rust, or decay is clearly visible and affects the appearance of the space. The signs should be noticeable and not just minor wear. Apply to neglected or deteriorated environments. Do not use for clean or well maintained spaces. This tag focuses on visible degradation and grime.
semantics.definition: Use when dirt, stains, mold, rust, or decay is clearly visible and affects the appearance of the space. The signs should be noticeable and not just minor wear. Apply to neglected or deteriorated environments. Do not use for clean or well maintained spaces. This tag focuses on visible degradation and grime.
examples_positive: Walls with visible grime and staining.
A space with rusted fixtures and decayed surfaces.
Clear evidence of Visible Dirt / Decay is present.
examples_negative: A clean room with no visible stains.
A well maintained lobby.
No clear evidence of Visible Dirt / Decay is present.
scope_includes: stains and grime
rust or corrosion
mold growth
visible decay
scope_excludes: clean surfaces
minor scuffs
well maintained finishes
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for discoloration, stains, rust, or patchy surfaces. Use texture analysis to detect grime and irregular surface patterns. Confirm that dirt is visible across meaningful areas. Avoid tagging when dirt is minimal or ambiguous.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material condition layers or defect detection to identify dirt and decay. Tag when defect density exceeds a threshold on visible surfaces. Consider both structural elements and furnishings.

env.ae.visible_hvac_vents.definition: Article Eater antecedent: Visible HVAC Vents

semantics.definition_short: HVAC vents, grilles, or diffusers are visible and identifiable in the scene.
semantics.definition_long: Use when mechanical air vents or grilles are clearly visible on ceilings, walls, or floors. The vents should be identifiable as HVAC elements rather than generic patterns. Apply to spaces where such fixtures are part of the visual field. Do not use when vents are hidden or too small to confirm. This tag is about visibility of mechanical ventilation.
semantics.definition: Use when mechanical air vents or grilles are clearly visible on ceilings, walls, or floors. The vents should be identifiable as HVAC elements rather than generic patterns. Apply to spaces where such fixtures are part of the visual field. Do not use when vents are hidden or too small to confirm. This tag is about visibility of mechanical ventilation.
examples_positive: A ceiling with multiple rectangular air diffusers.
A wall with a large ventilation grille.
Clear evidence of Visible HVAC Vents is present.
examples_negative: A ceiling with only recessed lights and no vents.
A space where vents are hidden behind panels.
No clear evidence of Visible HVAC Vents is present.

wall grilles
floor vents

scope_excludes: hidden HVAC systems

semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Use object detection for vent or grille shapes and check context near ceilings or walls. Rectangular or circular diffusers with slats are typical cues. Confirm scale and placement consistent with HVAC. Avoid false positives on speakers or lighting.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use object metadata for mechanical fixtures and mesh labels. Tag when HVAC outlet objects are visible in the view. Consider normal orientation and placement on ceilings or walls to validate.

semantics.definition_short: Windows are clearly visible as openings or glazed elements in the scene.
semantics.definition_long: Use when windows are present and identifiable, regardless of size or openness. The tag indicates visible window elements such as frames, panes, or glazing. Apply even if the windows are small or closed. Do not use if no window elements are visible. This tag is about visibility, not area.
semantics.definition: Use when windows are present and identifiable, regardless of size or openness. The tag indicates visible window elements such as frames, panes, or glazing. Apply even if the windows are small or closed. Do not use if no window elements are visible. This tag is about visibility, not area.
examples_positive: A room with multiple windows along one wall.
A corridor with a window at the end.
Clear evidence of Visible Windows is present.
examples_negative: A windowless interior room.
A space with only interior glass partitions and no windows.

window panes

scope_excludes: glass partitions without exterior connection

semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use scene graph to identify window objects and their visibility in the camera frustum. Tag when window meshes are within view. Distinguish windows from glass partitions by checking exterior adjacency.

env.ae.warm_color_palette.definition: Article Eater antecedent: Warm Color Palette

semantics.definition_short: The overall palette is dominated by warm hues such as reds, oranges, and yellows.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the dominant colors are warm and create a warm visual impression. This is about overall palette, not just lighting temperature. Apply when warm colors cover large surfaces or decor. Do not use if the palette is balanced or cool. This tag is about hue dominance.
semantics.definition: Use when the dominant colors are warm and create a warm visual impression. This is about overall palette, not just lighting temperature. Apply when warm colors cover large surfaces or decor. Do not use if the palette is balanced or cool. This tag is about hue dominance.
examples_positive: A room with terracotta walls and warm wood tones.
A space with orange and red decor as the main colors.
Clear evidence of Warm Color Palette is present.
examples_negative: A blue-green dominated space.
A neutral grey and white room.
No clear evidence of Warm Color Palette is present.
scope_includes: warm wall colors
warm-toned decor
red and orange surfaces
golden hues
scope_excludes: cool palettes
neutral monochrome
small warm accents only
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Compute hue distribution and verify a dominance of warm hue ranges. Confirm that warm colors are present across large areas, not just accents. Avoid tagging when warm lighting casts warm tone but surfaces are cool. Use color segmentation for major surfaces.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Aggregate material colors and compute warm hue proportion. Tag when warm hues exceed a threshold across visible surfaces. Consider lighting influence separately from material color.

notes.extraction_notes: Seed mapping exists in repo cnfa_crosswalk.yaml

semantics.definition_short: Lighting appears warm, with a yellow or amber color temperature.
semantics.definition_long: Use when illumination has a warm cast, typical of incandescent or warm LED lighting. Whites and neutrals should appear slightly yellow or amber. Apply when the lighting temperature is clearly warm across the scene. Do not use when lighting is cool or neutral. This tag concerns light color, not surface color.
semantics.definition: Use when illumination has a warm cast, typical of incandescent or warm LED lighting. Whites and neutrals should appear slightly yellow or amber. Apply when the lighting temperature is clearly warm across the scene. Do not use when lighting is cool or neutral. This tag concerns light color, not surface color.
examples_positive: A restaurant lit by warm pendant lights with amber glow.
A living room with warm lamps casting yellow light.
Clear evidence of Warm Lighting is present.
examples_negative: A corridor lit by cool white fluorescent lights.
Daylight with neutral or cool cast.

scope_includes: warm LED lighting
incandescent lighting
amber light fixtures
warm ambient light
scope_excludes: cool lighting
neutral daylight
colored lighting unrelated to warmth
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Estimate illuminant color from neutral surfaces and check for yellow or amber shifts. Warm lighting shows higher red channel relative to blue in whites. Confirm that the cast is consistent across the frame. Avoid tagging when warmth comes from surface colors rather than light.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use light source color temperature metadata where available. Tag when dominant lights have low color temperature values. Validate by sampling rendered white surfaces for warm bias.

semantics.definition_short: The space appears maintained and in good repair, with minimal visible wear or damage.
semantics.definition_long: Use when surfaces, fixtures, and furnishings show little to no visible damage, stains, or deterioration. This is about condition and upkeep rather than cleanliness alone. Apply when the overall impression is cared-for and functional. Do not use if there are notable cracks, peeling paint, or broken fixtures. This tag can co-occur with high_cleanliness but is distinct.
semantics.definition: Use when surfaces, fixtures, and furnishings show little to no visible damage, stains, or deterioration. This is about condition and upkeep rather than cleanliness alone. Apply when the overall impression is cared-for and functional. Do not use if there are notable cracks, peeling paint, or broken fixtures. This tag can co-occur with high_cleanliness but is distinct.
examples_positive: A hallway with intact finishes and no visible damage.
A stairwell with clean, unbroken handrails and walls.
Clear evidence of Well Maintained is present.
examples_negative: A room with peeling paint and broken tiles.
A space with damaged furniture and worn surfaces.
No clear evidence of Well Maintained is present.
scope_includes: intact finishes
working fixtures
undamaged walls and floors
no visible deterioration
scope_excludes: cracked or peeling surfaces
broken fixtures
visible neglect
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for cues of wear such as chipped paint, cracks, stains, or missing components. A consistent, intact finish across surfaces indicates good maintenance. Distinguish from cleanliness by focusing on damage rather than dirt. Avoid tagging if only a small area is maintained but others are damaged.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material condition annotations or defect detection models to identify damage. Tag when defect density is low across visible surfaces. Consider both structural elements and furniture condition.

semantics.definition_short: Wood is a dominant visible material in the scene.
semantics.definition_long: Use when wood surfaces cover a substantial portion of the visible area and strongly influence the material impression. This includes wood floors, wall panels, ceilings, or large furniture pieces. Apply when wood reads as a primary finish rather than minor accents. Do not use for small wooden items only. The tag focuses on material prominence.
semantics.definition: Use when wood surfaces cover a substantial portion of the visible area and strongly influence the material impression. This includes wood floors, wall panels, ceilings, or large furniture pieces. Apply when wood reads as a primary finish rather than minor accents. Do not use for small wooden items only. The tag focuses on material prominence.
examples_positive: A space with wood floors and wood wall paneling.
A room dominated by timber beams and wood ceiling.
Clear evidence of Wood Prominent is present.
examples_negative: A room with only a small wooden table.
A concrete space with minor wood trim.
No clear evidence of Wood Prominent is present.
scope_includes: wood floors
wood wall panels
timber ceilings
exposed timber structure
scope_excludes: small wood accents
wood patterns on non-wood materials
minor wooden furniture
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for wood grain textures and warm brown hues across large surfaces. Confirm that wood covers a significant area, not just small objects. Use material classification to distinguish wood from wood-look finishes when possible. Avoid tagging if wood is limited to trim.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material metadata to identify wood surfaces and compute visible area proportion. Tag when wood surfaces exceed a threshold of the view. Combine with texture cues such as grain patterns to validate.

env.ae.work_surfaces_present.definition: Article Eater antecedent: Work Surfaces Present

semantics.definition_short: Work surfaces such as desks, tables, or counters are visible and intended for tasks.
semantics.definition_long: Use when surfaces suitable for working, writing, or task activities are clearly visible. The surfaces should be functional and accessible, not just decorative consoles. Apply to offices, studios, labs, or work areas with desks or counters. Do not use when only small side tables or decorative shelves are present. This tag is about the presence of work-capable surfaces.
semantics.definition: Use when surfaces suitable for working, writing, or task activities are clearly visible. The surfaces should be functional and accessible, not just decorative consoles. Apply to offices, studios, labs, or work areas with desks or counters. Do not use when only small side tables or decorative shelves are present. This tag is about the presence of work-capable surfaces.
examples_positive: An office with desks and chairs arranged for work.
A workshop with long workbenches.
Clear evidence of Work Surfaces Present is present.
examples_negative: A lounge with only coffee tables.
A hallway with a narrow decorative console.
No clear evidence of Work Surfaces Present is present.
scope_includes: desks and work tables
work benches
task counters
lab work surfaces
scope_excludes: small side tables
decorative consoles
surfaces not intended for work
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Detect desk or table objects and verify scale consistent with working surfaces. Work surfaces often have chairs, monitors, or task tools nearby. Confirm accessibility and placement for use. Avoid tagging from tiny tables used for decor.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use object categories and dimensions to identify work surfaces. Tag when at least one desk or workbench is visible and accessible. Use proximity to task objects to reinforce classification.

semantics.definition_short: Surfaces or furnishings show wear, damage, or degradation beyond normal use.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the space exhibits visible wear such as scuffs, peeling paint, broken fixtures, or degraded materials. The wear should be noticeable and affect the overall impression. Apply when deterioration is more than minor scuffs. Do not use for clean, well maintained interiors. This tag overlaps with visible_dirt/decay but focuses on wear and damage.
semantics.definition: Use when the space exhibits visible wear such as scuffs, peeling paint, broken fixtures, or degraded materials. The wear should be noticeable and affect the overall impression. Apply when deterioration is more than minor scuffs. Do not use for clean, well maintained interiors. This tag overlaps with visible_dirt/decay but focuses on wear and damage.
examples_positive: A hallway with peeling paint and chipped edges.
A room with torn upholstery and broken tiles.
Clear evidence of Worn / Degraded is present.
examples_negative: A freshly renovated space with intact finishes.
A clean room with no visible damage.
No clear evidence of Worn / Degraded is present.
scope_includes: peeling paint
chipped surfaces
broken fixtures
torn upholstery
scope_excludes: minor scuffs
clean finishes
well maintained surfaces
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for visible damage patterns such as chips, cracks, or fraying. Ensure that wear is noticeable across meaningful areas. Distinguish wear from dirt by focusing on physical damage. Avoid tagging if wear is minimal or localized.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use defect detection or material condition metadata to identify damaged areas. Tag when damage coverage exceeds a threshold. Consider both structural surfaces and furniture integrity.

semantics.definition_short: Water elements such as fountains, pools, or streams are visible as part of the environment.
semantics.definition_long: Use when a water feature is clearly present and intended as an architectural or landscape element. This includes fountains, reflecting pools, indoor water walls, or visible streams. The water should be a visible, designed feature, not incidental moisture. Apply to indoor or outdoor scenes where water is a focal or notable element. Do not use for rain, wet floors, or small containers.
semantics.definition: Use when a water feature is clearly present and intended as an architectural or landscape element. This includes fountains, reflecting pools, indoor water walls, or visible streams. The water should be a visible, designed feature, not incidental moisture. Apply to indoor or outdoor scenes where water is a focal or notable element. Do not use for rain, wet floors, or small containers.
examples_positive: A lobby with a central fountain and water basin.
A courtyard with a reflecting pool.
Computed metric indicates Water features.
examples_negative: A wet floor after cleaning.
A small glass of water on a table.
Metric not computed for Water features.
scope_includes: fountains
pools and basins
water walls
streams in designed landscapes
scope_excludes: rain or puddles
wet surfaces only
small containers of water
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for water surfaces with reflections, ripples, or visible basins. Identify fountain structures, jets, or water walls. Confirm that water is part of the built environment. Avoid false positives from glossy floors or mirrors.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use scene graph objects tagged as water or fountain features. Check material properties such as reflectance and animated surfaces. Tag when water objects occupy a meaningful visible area or are central features.

env.v2a_070.prospect_refuge_ability_to_s.canonical_name: Prospect–refuge: ability to see without being seen
env.v2a_070.prospect_refuge_ability_to_s.definition: Prospect–refuge: ability to see without being seen; corner refuge availability.

search_terms: Prospect–refuge: ability to see without being seen

semantics.definition_short: A vantage point affords a clear view outward while the observer is partially concealed or protected.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the scene provides both **prospect** (good outward visibility) and **refuge** (a sense of cover, enclosure, or shielding) such that one could look out without feeling fully exposed. This is an affordance-level tag: it can be implied by geometry and placement of boundaries even if no person is present.
semantics.definition: Use when the scene provides both **prospect** (good outward visibility) and **refuge** (a sense of cover, enclosure, or shielding) such that one could look out without feeling fully exposed. This is an affordance-level tag: it can be implied by geometry and placement of boundaries even if no person is present.
examples_positive: A booth along a wall looking across a restaurant with high-backed seating providing cover.
A window seat in an alcove with a view to the street while side walls/overhang provide concealment.
A mezzanine behind a balustrade overlooking a lobby; railing + setback provide partial protection.
examples_negative: A completely open plaza with long views but no cover (prospect only).
A small enclosed room with no meaningful outward view (refuge only).
Metric not computed for Prospect–refuge: ability to see without being seen.
scope_includes: alcoves, niches, bays with views
overhangs/canopies at overlook points
high-backed seating/booths
balustrades/railings that allow viewing
setbacks behind columns/screens
scope_excludes: purely open views with no shielding
fully enclosed spaces without outward view
privacy screens that block viewing outward
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for partial enclosures around a viewing direction (walls/columns/screens behind or beside the viewpoint) + an open view corridor ahead. In 2D images: strong depth cues (vanishing lines) plus nearby boundary elements framing the viewer position. CV hint: estimate visible depth/line-of-sight corridor; detect nearby vertical boundaries within ~1–3m of camera viewpoint (columns/walls) while distant region remains visible.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Compute isovist / viewshed from candidate occupant positions and combine with refuge metric (nearby occluding surfaces behind/beside). In VR/3D: ray-cast visibility vs proximity-to-cover; flag nodes with high outward visibility + high local enclosure.

env.v2a_098.natural_materials_wood_stone.definition: Natural materials: wood/stone; biomorphic forms; nature images.

semantics.definition_short: Natural materials such as wood and stone are prominently visible in the space.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the scene features substantial areas of natural wood and stone finishes, giving an organic material character. Both materials should be visible and notable, not just one small accent. Apply when wood and stone are primary materials for floors, walls, or major elements. Do not use when surfaces are synthetic imitations or only one material is present. This tag is about natural material presence.
semantics.definition: Use when the scene features substantial areas of natural wood and stone finishes, giving an organic material character. Both materials should be visible and notable, not just one small accent. Apply when wood and stone are primary materials for floors, walls, or major elements. Do not use when surfaces are synthetic imitations or only one material is present. This tag is about natural material presence.
examples_positive: A lobby with a stone floor and wood wall paneling.
A space with a stone feature wall and timber ceiling beams.
Computed metric indicates Natural materials: wood / stone.
examples_negative: A room with wood furniture but no stone surfaces.
A space with stone tile but no visible wood.
Metric not computed for Natural materials: wood / stone.
scope_includes: stone walls or floors
wood panels or beams
natural stone and timber finishes
combined wood and stone surfaces
scope_excludes: synthetic stone look
wood or stone alone
minor accents only
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Detect wood grain and stone textures on large surfaces. Confirm presence of both materials within the same scene. Use material classifiers to reduce confusion with printed or synthetic finishes. Avoid tagging if one material is only a small accent.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material tags for wood and stone and compute visible area coverage for each. Tag when both materials exceed minimum coverage thresholds. Validate with surface texture properties and reflectance characteristics.
